patient.name: Connor Walsh
patient.chiefComplaint: Pulled from a lake after estimated 4-6 minute submersion

history.hpi: 17-year-old male pulled from Lake Harmon by his friends after an estimated 4-6 minutes of submersion in cold water. Group of teenagers were jumping off a dock into the lake. Witnesses report he hit the water and did not resurface. Friends pulled him out after approximately 5 minutes. Water temperature is approximately 12°C (54°F). Bystanders started rescue breaths on shore.
history.lastOralIntake: Food/snacks on the dock before jumping (approximate)
history.events: Teenager jumped from dock into cold lake, did not resurface, pulled out after estimated 4-6 minutes of submersion. Rescue breathing initiated on shore by friends.

scene.dispatch: Medic 15, respond code 3 to Lake Harmon public dock on Beach Road, for a 17-year-old male, submersion, now on shore.
scene.location: Public swimming dock at a lake. Patient on the dock, wet, on a towel. Ambulance can pull up to the parking lot 50 feet away.
scene.safety: Scene safe now that patient is on shore. Crowd of teenagers, parent arriving.
scene.bystanders: Multiple teenage friends, one performing rescue breaths. Witnesses report 4-6 minute submersion.
visualCues: Adolescent male supine on a towel on the dock, soaking wet
No obvious neck injury, did not dive head-first per witnesses

phases.name: Arrival — Submersion Victim with Gasping Respirations
phases.description: Adolescent drowning victim. ABC approach (not CAB) is critical in drowning — airway and breathing come first because the primary insult is hypoxic. The patient has spontaneous gasping respirations and a pulse but is hypoxic and bradycardic. Cold water exposure adds hypothermia to manage. Mechanism (jumping, not diving) makes c-spine injury less likely but should still be considered.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Patent but with coughing and water in the oropharynx
clinicalPresentation.breathing: Labored gasping at 12/min with poor tidal volume
otherFindings: Mechanism (jumping, not diving) makes c-spine injury less likely but should be assessed
Cold water submersion with hypothermia — core temperature approximately 33°C
Witnessed submersion time 4-6 minutes — survivable range
Bystander rescue breaths initiated on shore — good prognostic sign

expectedActions.action: Use ABC approach (airway → breathing → circulation), not CAB — drowning is a hypoxic insult that requires prompt oxygenation and ventilation
expectedActions.rationale: Drowning pathophysiology is primary hypoxia. Starting with ventilation (ABC) is standard even during rescue breathing on scene.

expectedActions.action: Suction airway of water and secretions; do NOT attempt to expel water with abdominal thrusts or positioning — risks aspiration
Begin 2-person BVM with PEEP 5 cmH2O and 100% oxygen to correct profound hypoxemia; support spontaneous respirations
Mechanism was jumping (not diving), so c-spine injury is less likely; assess for any posterior tenderness or deformity and use clinical judgment — provide manual stabilization if uncertain
Remove wet clothing, dry the patient, and apply warm blankets to limit ongoing heat loss
Obtain core temperature (rectal if available) to guide hypothermia management
Apply cardiac monitor — hypothermic myocardium is prone to dysrhythmia with rough handling
Move the patient gently — hypothermic myocardium can be precipitated into VF by jostling

phases.name: Default Path — Improvement with Oxygenation and Warming
phases.description: With aggressive BVM support, drying, and warming, the patient's SpO2 climbs, HR comes up, and consciousness improves. He may be able to be managed with high-flow oxygen or CPAP instead of continued BVM. Still requires transport for observation.
phases.triggerCondition: Clinical improvement with oxygenation and warming

clinicalPresentation.airway: Patent, maintaining own airway with protective cough
clinicalPresentation.breathing: Improved — spontaneous respirations at 22/min with some rales on auscultation (early water exposure)

skin.color: improving — central pink, still peripherally cool

otherFindings: Rales suggest some water aspiration — set up for possible post-immersion pulmonary edema

expectedActions.action: Consider CPAP starting at 5 cmH2O to treat immersion pulmonary edema if patient tolerates and mental status permits
Continue passive warming — warm blankets, heat on in ambulance; avoid aggressive rewarming in the field
Watch cardiac monitor for hypothermic dysrhythmias (J-waves, AFib, bradycardia, VF risk)
Transport to ED — all drowning victims must be transported even if they look okay (delayed pulmonary edema, secondary drowning)

phases.name: Branch: Deterioration Requiring RSI
phases.description: Patient fails to improve with BVM and develops worsening hypoxemia from aspiration/pulmonary edema. Mental status deteriorates. Crew must escalate to RSI, being mindful of the cold myocardium.
phases.triggerCondition: Failure to improve with aggressive BVM and supportive care

otherFindings: Worsening pulmonary edema with pink frothy sputum on BVM

expectedActions.action: Perform RSI with Ketamine 1-2 mg/kg IV (100 mg for 68 kg patient) + Rocuronium 1 mg/kg (68 mg); be gentle during laryngoscopy to avoid precipitating VF in the cold myocardium
Confirm tube placement with waveform capnography, bilateral breath sounds
Ventilate with PEEP 5-8 cmH2O to treat immersion pulmonary edema
Emergent transport to closest appropriate ED

criticalActions: Use ABC approach (Airway-Breathing-Circulation), not CAB, for drowning
Suction airway of water without abdominal thrusts
Begin 2-person BVM with PEEP and high-flow oxygen
Remove wet clothing and apply warm blankets
Handle the hypothermic patient gently to avoid precipitating VF
Apply cardiac monitor and watch for hypothermic dysrhythmias
Consider CPAP for immersion pulmonary edema if mental status permits
Transport all drowning victims for observation
expectedActions: Consider c-spine precautions based on mechanism
Document submersion time and water temperature
bonusActions: Recognize that bradycardia + cold patient should not be treated with atropine (physiologic response to hypothermia)

learningObjectives: Apply MATC Drowning protocol including ABC approach
Manage the hypothermic drowning patient without precipitating VF
Recognize immersion pulmonary edema and use CPAP as treatment
Understand when c-spine precautions are indicated in drowning
Document submersion time and water temperature for termination-of-resuscitation decisions
discussionQuestions: Why is the approach to drowning ABC instead of CAB?
Why are abdominal thrusts harmful in a drowning victim?
How does cold water affect survival and resuscitation decisions?
When do you immobilize the c-spine in a drowning victim?
What is 'secondary drowning' and why do we transport apparently well patients?
commonPitfalls: Starting with compressions (CAB) instead of ventilation in arrest
Attempting to expel water with abdominal thrusts
Rough handling of a hypothermic patient precipitating VF
Releasing apparently well patients without transport
keyTakeaways: Drowning is a hypoxic problem — fix the airway first
All drowning victims transport — secondary edema is real
Submersion time and water temperature drive termination decisions
